content.problemStatement:
Well, before Chef could counter attack, Dr Doof showed his innocent face which moved Chef. Moreover, on hearing his complete story, Chef took pity on him and decided to befriend him. He even decided to play a game with him. The game goes as follows:

Chef gives Dr Doof a string SSS of length NNN consisting only of lowercase Latin letters and asks him to study the string carefully.

A substring of a string is a contiguous subsequence of that string. We denote the substring of string SSS starting from the lthl^{\text{th}}lth character and ending with the rthr^{\text{th}}rth character as S[l,r]S[l, r]S[l,r]. So, strings chef and odech are substrings of string codechef, but string codee is not.

Chef defines a substring to be **unique** if it occurs in the whole string SSS exactly once.

After Dr Doof studies the string, Chef asks him QQQ queries of the form - LLL RRR KKK.

For each query, you have to find the lexicographically smallest **unique** substring of length atleast KKK in [L,R][L, R][L,R], or say that there is no **unique** substring in this range. Formally, the answer to each query is two integers lll and rrr such that:


L‚Č§l‚Č§r‚Č§RL \leq l \leq r \leq RL‚Č§l‚Č§r‚Č§R
r‚ąíl+1‚Č•Kr - l + 1 \geq Kr‚ąíl+1‚Č•K
S[l,r]S[l, r]S[l,r] is the lexicographically smallest **unique** substring satisfying conditions 111 and 222.
String ppp is lexicographically smaller than string qqq, if ppp is a prefix of qqq not equal to qqq or there exists an index iii, such that pip_{i}pi‚Äč‚ÄČ<‚ÄČqiq_{i}qi‚Äč and for all j‚ÄČ<‚ÄČi,pj‚ÄČ=‚ÄČqjj‚ÄČ<‚ÄČi, p_{j}‚ÄČ=‚ÄČq_{j}j‚ÄČ<‚ÄČi,pj‚Äč‚ÄČ=‚ÄČqj‚Äč. For example, abc is lexicographically smaller than abcd , abd is lexicographically smaller than abec.

This time, you have to help the poor Dr Doof answer the queries as he doesn't want to lose his new friend. If there is no **unique** substring S[l,r]S[l, r]S[l,r] satisfying the above conditions, print two space separated integers: ‚ąí1-1‚ąí1 ‚ąí1-1‚ąí1.

###Input:


The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The second line contains a single string SSS.
The third line contains a single integer QQQ.
QQQ lines follow. Each of these lines contains three space-separated integers LLL RRR KKK describing a query.
###Output:
For each query, print a single line containing two space separated integers lll and rrr ‚Äē the answer to the query.

###Constraints


1‚Č§T‚Č§1051 \leq T \leq 10^51‚Č§T‚Č§105
2‚Č§N‚Č§5√ó1052 \leq N \leq 5\times10^52‚Č§N‚Č§5√ó105
1‚Č§Q‚Č§5√ó1051 \leq Q \leq 5 \times 10^51‚Č§Q‚Č§5√ó105
1‚Č§L‚Č§R‚Č§N1 \leq L \leq R \leq N1‚Č§L‚Č§R‚Č§N
1‚Č§K‚Č§R‚ąíL+11 \leq K \leq R - L + 11‚Č§K‚Č§R‚ąíL+1
The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 5√ó1055\times10^55√ó105
The sum of QQQ over all test cases does not exceed 5√ó1055\times10^55√ó105
###Sample Input:
1
5
abcab
4
1 3 2
2 5 3
3 4 1
4 5 1

###Sample Output:
1 3
2 4
3 3
-1 -1

###Explanation:

Query 111: The unique substrings in [1,3][1, 3][1,3] are c, abc and bc. abc is the lexicographically smallest unique substring of length atleast 2.

Query 222: The unique substrings in [2,5][2, 5][2,5] are bc, bca, bcab, c, ca and cab. bca is the lexicographically smallest unique substring of length atleast 3.

Query 333: The unique substrings in [3,4][3, 4][3,4] are c and ca. c is the lexicographically smallest unique substring of length atleast 1.

Query 444: None of the substrings in [4,5][4,5][4,5] are unique.
